content: Has anybody touched TC or SKB yet? Jamal responded and suggested that a rust clone of https://elixir.bootlin.com/linux/v6.8-rc1/source/net/sched/act_simple.c could be a good place to start here
I think that's on the table for someone looking for a project. I might pick it up in a couple weeks if nobody else does

Thanks for asking, I was thinking about networking for my first project. As of now, it would have probably been a TCP congestion control algorithm (e.g. reimplementing dctcp, hybla, or bic). Since nobody _asked_ for that to be done I am wondering if that topic would be more useful. Any opinions?
Hi Trevor, I remember the discussion in the net meeting; I would love to work on TC so I am absolutely available to pick it up.
Forgot to link, here is the rest of the context https://lore.kernel.org/rust-for-linux/CALNs47sqEzW831Sjh7WzgaVrLQJmM9b0=8bhkWLrR3592GU4vg@mail.gmail.com/T/#t

Michele Dalle Rive said:

Hi Trevor, I remember the discussion in the net meeting; I would love to work on TC so I am absolutely available to pick it up.

Awesome! In that case, I'm happy to help if needed

Valentin Obst said:

Thanks for asking, I was thinking about networking for my first project. As of now, it would have probably been a TCP congestion control algorithm (e.g. reimplementing dctcp, hybla, or bic). Since nobody _asked_ for that to be done I am wondering if that topic would be more useful. Any opinions?

I don't think nobody asking means it's not useful :smile:

The trickiest thing for everything RFL is that nothing can make it into tree without a use case, and duplicates of existing functionality are frowned upon. So it's tricky to find projects that are useful, but also low enough priority that there is time to implement them in Rust and also live with our current platform restrictions
Having working rewrites opens doors for future work though, so I think it's easily worth it even if you can't expect the rewrites to get in (as is the case for TC here)
Doesn't hurt to reach out to the TCP maintainer (Eric Dumazet + lists) to gauge interest

Trevor Gross said:

Doesn't hurt to reach out to the TCP maintainer (Eric Dumazet + lists) to gauge interest

Should i also cc the rfl lust and maintainers?

duplicates of existing functionality are frowned upon.

Maybe I also find a CCA paper that has no upstream implementation yet :grinning:

Valentin Obst said:

Should i also cc the rfl lust and maintainers?

I think it's always good to hit related lists (so rfl as well as the relevant subsystem list https://github.com/torvalds/linux/blob/615d300648869c774bd1fe54b4627bb0c20faed4/MAINTAINERS#L15339-L15341), but there isn't any reason to specifically cc Rust maintainers if your mail isn't primarily intended for them.

I would just express your interest and ask if he is open to having a Rust CCA in tree \someday\, maybe has specific ideas for a project. Tread a bit carefully, more maintainers are becoming more open to Rust but quite a few are pretty against it still :smile:

Maybe I also find a CCA paper that has no upstream implementation yet :grinning:

That's probably the best eventual path forward for something in tree smile: Eric might be able to point you in a good direction

Valentin Obst said:

Should i also cc the rfl lust and maintainers?

If it is a public email, then yeah, it would be nice to Cc the list. (In fact, for patches in particular, for a similar reason, we ask to please Cc the list and maintainers for the time being even if they are not patches intended for us. It is an easy way to keep anybody interested in Rust in the loop.)

If it is private, then please feel free to Cc us if you want (please see the notes at https://rust-for-linux.com/contributing#submitting-new-abstractions-and-modules).
